coredata.dc:description: Fungi are the prime concern since they cause post-harvest decay in fruits such as strawberries; the fungi motivate the need for new antifungal agents. Among all these nanoparticles, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) show the most promising results for their use in food packaging applications. Lemon carbon quantum dots (LCQD), which is derived from lemon juice, and onion carbon quantum dots (OCQD), which is derived from onion juice, were synthesized using the one-step hydrothermal method. The synthesized materials were then characterized using a transmission electron microscope, X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy. LCQD and OCQD solutions were applied to fresh strawberries for the purpose of conducting studies on weight loss, decay, and shelf-life extension. The antifungal activity of both CQDs was assessed against Rhizopus sp., Penicillium sp., Candida albicans, Aspergillus sp., and Botrytis cinerea using the agar-well diffusion technique. The obtained average size was 4.33 ± 1.67 nm for LCQDs and 3.34 ± 1.72 nm for OCQDs. Strawberries packaged with OCQDs and LCQDs showed weight loss of 5.21% ± 1.03% and 8.14% ± 1.09% on the fourth day of observation, respectively, against 18.71% ± 2.2% by control. On the same day, the decay rate for OCQDs was 11%, 40% for LCQDs, and 96.65% for control. Both exhibited antifungal activity, with inhibition zones ranging from 12.6 to 44.5 mm for various fungi. OCQD exhibits higher activity packaging compared to LCQD due to their smaller size and superior antifungal activity against a variety of fungi.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a schematic depicting the hydrothermal synthesis of LCQDs and OCQDs derived from lemon and onion juice.
Depicts TEM images displaying the size distribution of OCQDs and LCQDs prepared using straightforward hydrothermal techniques.
XRD patterns of LCQDs and OCQDs prepared hydrothermally from lemon and onion juice.
FTIR spectra of the LCQDs and OCQDs prepared hydrothermally from lemon and onion juice.
Depicts the UV–Vis spectra of LCQDs and OCQDs prepared hydrothermally from lemon and onion juice.

A Tauc plot derived from UV–Vis absorption data for OCQD and LCQD, featuring a direct band gap blue line and an indirect band gap black line. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
a) Physiological weight loss and b) decay tests of unpackaged strawberries (control) and those packaged with LCQD and OCQD during four days of storage.
The appearance of fresh, LCQD-packaged, and OCQD-packaged strawberries during the 96-h storage period.
Illustrates the presence of a distinct type of fungus on the strawberry fruit that was not treated with CQDs (control).
Depicts zones of inhibition of the antifungal activity of LCQD and OCQD prepared by the hydrothermal method, as determined by the well diffusion method.
The antibacterial activity of OCQD and LCQD against airborne suspension bacteria.
A comparison of the size and emission color of CQDs produced by distinct natural precursors under varying hydrothermal conditions.
Presents a comparison of the antifungal activity of OCQDs and LCQDs with that of various nanoparticles as reported in the literature.
Displays the antioxidant activity of LCQD and OCQD using the DPPH scavenging assay at various concentrations.

head.ce:title: Antifungal films for strawberry packaging using carbon quantum dots derived from lemon and onion juice via green hydrothermal method

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fungi are the prime concern since they cause post-harvest decay in fruits such as strawberries; the fungi motivate the need for new antifungal agents. Among all these nanoparticles, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) show the most promising results for their use in food packaging applications. Lemon carbon quantum dots (LCQD), which is derived from lemon juice, and onion carbon quantum dots (OCQD), which is derived from onion juice, were synthesized using the one-step hydrothermal method. The synthesized materials were then characterized using a transmission electron microscope, X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy. LCQD and OCQD solutions were applied to fresh strawberries for the purpose of conducting studies on weight loss, decay, and shelf-life extension. The antifungal activity of both CQDs was assessed against Rhizopus sp., Penicillium sp., Candida albicans, Aspergillus sp., and Botrytis cinerea using the agar-well diffusion technique. The obtained average size was 4.33 ± 1.67 nm for LCQDs and 3.34 ± 1.72 nm for OCQDs. Strawberries packaged with OCQDs and LCQDs showed weight loss of 5.21% ± 1.03% and 8.14% ± 1.09% on the fourth day of observation, respectively, against 18.71% ± 2.2% by control. On the same day, the decay rate for OCQDs was 11%, 40% for LCQDs, and 96.65% for control. Both exhibited antifungal activity, with inhibition zones ranging from 12.6 to 44.5 mm for various fungi. OCQD exhibits higher activity packaging compared to LCQD due to their smaller size and superior antifungal activity against a variety of fungi.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Significant economic losses are primarily attributed to the proliferation of fungal pathogens throughout the postharvest processing of fruits. Even when the most sophisticated postharvest technologies are implemented, Botrytis cinerea and Penicillium expansum are capable of causing severe postharvest fruit diseases, such as blue and gray mold (Kumar et al., 2022; Kumar & Gaikwad, 2023; Singh et al., 2021). This has motivated scientists to investigate several techniques for improving freshness, quality, shelf life, and food safety by using natural, edible, and biodegradable polymers (Kumar et al., 2022; Kumar & Gaikwad, 2023). Fungi have also developed resistance to numerous conventional fungicides, including dicarboximides and benzimidazoles, which further complicate efforts to regulate fungal growth. Exploring novel antifungal agents that may supplant existing control strategies is crucial for overcoming this resistance (Fisher et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2023).

There has been a surge in interest surrounding nanoparticle (NP) materials owing to their notable distinctions in chemical and physical characteristics compared to traditional materials (Ghadiri et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2023). Food packaging is facilitated by their antimicrobial properties; edible films composed of both inorganic and organic nanoparticles are feasible (Saravanakumar et al., 2020). Most food packaging utilizes inorganic nanoparticles (e.g., zinc oxide, silver oxide, and titanium oxide) to extend shelf life and prevent contaminated illness (Kondle et al., 2022; Saravanakumar et al., 2020). Organic nanoparticles, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are an innovative type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial. These particles typically have a spatial dimension below 10 nm and are laden with functional groups, including carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl (Ghoreishi et al., 2020; Mansuriya & Altintas, 2021).

The CQDs have captured the scientific community's interest with their outstanding qualities, including multicolor fluorescence, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, high stability, aqueous solubility, and catalytic performances (Atchudan et al., 2021; Mansuriya & Altintas, 2021; Yadav et al., 2023). Furthermore, an increasing need for carbon dots in food packaging has been observed, given that these active substances can be incorporated into food packaging with antioxidant or antimicrobial properties (Kumar et al., 2022; Kumar & Gaikwad, 2023). Numerous processes have been developed to synthesize CQDs from various precursors, including arc discharge, electrochemical oxidation, thermal treatment, laser ablation, ultrasonication, microwave treatment, and hydrothermal treatment (Khayal et al., 2021).

Recent efforts to build green synthetic procedures for the fabrication of CQDs have investigated the use of organic precursors. CQDs derived from food items and/or byproducts are deemed acceptable for biological applications since these natural carbon supplies have no known toxicity (Sahana et al., 2023). Some attempts have been made to synthesize CQDs from non-toxic carbon resources utilizing a one-step hydrothermal/solvothermal technology; for instance, garlic (Sun et al., 2016), apple juice (Yue et al., 2014), pomelo peel (Tarokh et al., 2021), carrot juice (Liu et al., 2017), and orange juice (Sahu et al., 2012) have been used as green sources to synthesize CQDs. This is because natural biomass is harmless, environmentally friendly, sustainable, inexpensive, readily available, and abundant (Liu et al., 2017; Tarokh et al., 2021). Compared to other high-temperature and low-temperature synthesis methods, the primary benefits of this one are the control over the morphology of the NPs and the minimal size variation of the NPs. This is done by optimizing the synthesis parameters, including the amount of precursors, temperature, pH, and reducing and stabilizing factors (Ocsoy et al., 2017). CQD may be produced by carbonizing onions, which are a high source of dietary fiber, alk (en)yl cysteine sulfoxides, and non-structural carbohydrates such as fructose, sucrose, glucose, and fructooligosaccharides (Bandi et al., 2016). Lemon, which contains organic acids and carbohydrates such as citric acid, ascorbic acid, glucose, sucrose, fructose, etc. as carbon precursors (Hoan et al., 2019). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are manufactured using natural materials and serve supplementary purposes in food packaging, thus enhancing food items' freshness, quality, and longevity (Kumar et al., 2022; Kumar & Gaikwad, 2023). Regarding the application of carbon dots to food safety and preservation, Qu et al. (2018) present a comprehensive analysis. Implementing these active substances enhances the barrier and strength characteristics of packaging. Researchers have created active packaging supplies that extend the storage life of fresh produce by incorporating carbon particles derived from various sources (Kumar et al., 2022). The study shows that incorporating carbon nanoparticles obtained from enoki mushrooms into gelatin- and carrageenan-based composite films enhances the film's antioxidant, mechanical strength, and UV-blocking properties without altering its transparency (Roy et al., 2021).

Strawberry is chosen as the primary fruit in this investigation to evaluate the prepared CQD in the context of food packaging. This is due to the fruit's widespread popularity worldwide and its nutritional composition, which comprises significant amounts of folate, vitamin C, potassium, and phenolic constituents. The study introduces a green, simple, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly method that synthesizes fluorescent carbon quantum dots from lemon and onion juices, resulting in two samples known as lemon carbon quantum dots (LCQDs) and onion carbon quantum dots (OCQDs), respectively, through hydrothermal treatment. In addition, the antifungal activity of the LCQD and OCQD against pathogenic fungi was evaluated using the agar-well diffusion technique. Furthermore, water-soluble LCQDs and OCQDs have been applied on fresh strawberries for fruit packaging, as explained in graphical abstract.

Lemons (Citric limon) and onions (Allium cepa Linnaeus) are utilized as raw materials to produce LCQD and OCQD, respectively, without any chemical additives. Strawberries (Fragaria ananassa Linnaeus) were harvested from a farm situated in Erbil, Iraq. The fungal specimens used in this investigation were obtained from the Department of Biology at Salahaddin University-Erbil, Iraq.

The potato dextrose agar, a solid culture medium, was acquired from Scharlau in Barcelona, Spain. The 1,1-diphenylpicrylhydrazyl (DPPH) free radical scavenging was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All chemical materials were of analytical grade and used without further purification. All solutions were freshly prepared using double-distilled water and kept in darkness to avoid any photochemical reactions. All glassware that was used in the process was washed with acetone and then with double-distilled water and dried before use.

Using a one-step hydrothermal method, two different types of fluorescent carbon quantum dots (LCQD and OCQD) were synthesized from lemon and onion juices. Fig. 1 shows the schematic illustrations of the hydrothermal procedures for CQD synthesis. Plants were peeled, washed multiple times with double-distilled water, and processed in cold press juicers to extract the juice. The juice underwent centrifugation at a temperature of 25 °C, rotating at a speed of 10,000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate a large particle. Then, 75 mL of the fresh extract (juice) was poured into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was then heated in an electric oven at 200 °C for 6 h and slowly cooled down to room temperature (25 °C). After the cooling process, the sample was filtered by a syringe filter with a 0.25 μm pore size and then centrifuged at a temperature of 25 °C, rotating at a speed of 10,000 rpm for 20 min. The prepared solutions of LCQD and OCQD have been stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C for future testing. The LCQD and OCQD were dried using freeze-drying, which involved evaporating them at a temperature of 6 °C in a refrigerator for a period of two days. A sponge-like substance with an approximate weight of 10 mg was produced.

The morphological characteristics of CQD were analyzed by employing a Zeiss FETEM Libra 120 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The particle size distribution was assessed by applying the Java-based software program Image-J (version 1.53 k, 2021), developed by the National Institutes of Health in Bethesda, Maryland, USA. The program was applied to the output images obtained from the TEM. The diameter distribution of particles in two equal regions of the TEM images selected for LCQD and OCQD is calculated using the oval and straight tools.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) apparatus (Burker D8-advanced) with Cu-Kα (λ = 1.54178 Å) radiation characterized the structure of the powdered LCQD and OCQD. The XRD was operated at 40 kV and 40 mA 2θ, with a sweeping motion of 0.02° across 10° and 80° (Etefa et al., 2023). The bonding characteristics of CQD structures were identified by employing Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (PerkinElmer Spectrum GX) on the absorption spectra spanning 400–8000 cm−1 (Hoan et al., 2019).

An ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrometer from PerkinElmer, USA, was utilized to measure the absorption spectra in the 200–800 nm range to study the optical properties. The Tauc plot method was then used to determine the optical band gap (Eg) for OCQD and LCQD based on the absorption spectra. The subsequent equations illustrate the Davis-Mott and Tauc relationships:
(1) (αhυ)m = A(hυ-Eg)

Where α is the absorption coefficient, hυ is the incident photon energy, m is the power factor used to fit the data set, which is 1/2 or 2 for direct and indirect allowed transitions, respectively, and “A" is an energy-independent constant (Adsetts et al., 2020). The Tauc plot is obtained by plotting (αhυ)m against energy in eV. The tangent line at α = 0 was then drawn to obtain Eg.

Fresh strawberries were harvested from a farm located in Erbil, Iraq, when they were brilliant crimson and at the same stage of ripeness (after 35 days of post-floral visual evaluation at 95% red). The fruits were chosen based on their homogeneous color, size, absence of parasites, and lack of blemishes, disease, and physical injury. To evaluate the efficacy of both LCQD and OCQD as fruit packaging materials, fresh strawberries (10 strawberries for each group) were sprayed with two different types of the prepared CQD using an airbrush nozzle (Airbrush HD-130 no. 302, Taiwan). The airbrush was 0.3 mm in diameter and had a 7-cc container capacity with a 4 mL/min flow rate. An amount of 0.5 mL from the prepared stock solution (100 ppm) of both LCQD and OCQD was applied to the surface of a fresh strawberry to test the effect of the packaging on extending the fruit's shelf life. Each piece of the strawberry has been put in separate plastic pouches, each of which is provided with a hole for ventilation. Then, the strawberries were stored in a dark environment at over 68% RH and 25–27 °C to accelerate the colonization and growth of mold on the samples. Physiological weight loss and decay tests were conducted to evaluate the quality of the strawberries during the shelf-life study. The decrease in weight of strawberries was assessed by measuring the difference between their starting weight and their weight measured using a precise electronic balance (SartIrius, with an accuracy of 0.0001 g) on certain days (0, 1, 2, 3, and 4). The quantification of weight loss was determined as a percentage relative to the original weight measured at day 0 of storage. Simultaneously, while evaluating weight loss, the decay % of strawberries was assessed by recording the number of strawberries exhibiting surface mycelia growth as the decaying ones. The decay percentage for each treatment was determined by dividing the number of decayed strawberries by the original number of all fruit and then multiplying the result by 100 (Khodaei et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determine the occurrence and frequency of fungal genera on strawberries
ce:section.ce:para: Ten strawberries covered with fungi from the control group were stored in clean plastic bags, taken to the College of Sciences at the University of Salahaddin, and kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C for a few hours before analysis. The tissue was cut from the advancing margin of the lesion, and the surface was washed in sterile distilled water. Then, the tissue was dried on sterile filter paper on a clean bench, plated on sterile fresh medium potato dextrose agar (PDA), and incubated at 28 °C for ten days. The fungal samples were transferred to sterilized plates for purification and identification (Gupta et al., 2020).

To identify the fungal genera, the fungi were mounted on a slide, stained with lactophenol cotton blue to detect fungal structures, covered with a cover slip, examined under a microscope, and identified based on their colony morphology and spore characteristics. The identification of fungi relies on taxonomic keys found in specific texts (Gautam & Avasthi, 2019). The frequency of fungal occurrence was measured by counting the number of colonies on each plate and calculating the percentage of each fungus's occurrence frequency in each sample (Algabr et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antifungal activity of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The various fungal specimens included in this investigation were acquired from the Department of Biology at Salahaddin University-Erbil, Iraq. The fungal isolation and identification process determines the species, which involves analyzing the colony's features and the microscopic morphology and structure following (Chen et al., 2019). In this study, Rhizopus sp., Penicillium sp., Candida albicans, Aspergillus sp., and Botrytis cinerea fungi were chosen to assess the antifungal properties of LCQD and OCQD, depending on the agar-well diffusion technique. Rhizopus sp. and Aspergillus sp. are sourced from soil. At the same time, Candida sp. is obtained from a cervical vulvovaginal patient, and Botrytis cinerea is isolated from an infected strawberry during this investigation. Fungi culture was distributed over a Potato Dextrose Agar (PDA) plate; wells were punctured into the agar. The stock solution for both LCQDs and OCQDs prepared had a concentration of 100 ppm. Then, 25 μL of LCQDs and OCQDs were injected into each of the wells. The samples were then incubated for 48 h at 37 °C. Antifungal activity is determined by measuring the diameter of an inhibition zone surrounding the sample, which appears as a transparent area surrounding the wells. The average diameter of the inhibitory zone was determined using Image-J (version 1.53 k, 2021), a software program developed by the National Institutes of Health in Bethesda, Maryland, USA. The significant value was recorded in millimeters for each fungal species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DPPH assay for scavenging free radicals
ce:section.ce:para:
The DPPH free radical scavenging assay was conducted following a specific protocol. 1 mL of a 100 μM ethanolic DPPH solution was mixed with various volumes of the prepared stock solution of different LCQD and OCQD. The solution was agitated and left in darkness for different periods. The absorbance at 517 nm was then recorded to quantify the concentration of the remaining free DPPH* radical. The tests were conducted three times, and Deionized Millipore water (MQW) was utilized as a blank control. The efficiency of scavenging DPPH* by the three types of CQDs were determined using the equation below (Chen et al., 2019; Roy et al., 2022):
(2) DPPH inhibition % = [(Ac−Asa) × 100/Ac]
Where Ac is the absorbance of the control and Asa is the absorbance of the sample.

To evaluate the antifungal efficacy of OCQD and LCQD against airborne fungi, an air sampling procedure was implemented utilizing three 90-mm settle plates containing potato dextrose agar (PDA) medium. The plates were exposed to the air within a sealed box (100 × 60 × 30 cm) for 6 h. Four milliliters of each LCQD and OCQD solution were sprayed into the box during this process. After three days of incubation at 28 °C, the plates were examined daily for visible fungal growth (Wang et al., 2022).

The data were analyzed utilizing GraphPad Prism® version 9.5.0 software (GraphPad Software, USA) in conjunction with a two-way ANOVA. A significance level of P < 0.001 was defined as statistically significant. Furthermore, the findings are displayed as means and standard deviations (SD) for three separate investigations, with ten strawberries assigned to each group (El-Hadary et al., 2023).

To investigate the particle size and shape of LCQD and OCQD prepared using lemon and onion juices, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 2 , TEM images of the samples were acquired. On the right side of Fig. 2, the proportional distribution of particulate size is depicted. The TEM images of CQD disclose a uniform distribution of spherical particles in terms of size. The percentage distribution of particle size indicates that the average size of LCQDs is 4.33 ± 1.67 nm, and for OCQDs, it is 3.34 ± 1.72 nm. Other researchers utilizing hydrothermal treatment on other plants have reported a different size of CQDs and a different color emission as they exited under UV light, as shown in Table 1 .

The structure and nature of functional groups bonded to the surface of carbon quantum dots have been analyzed utilizing XRD and FTIR. Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of both LCQDs and OCQDs. The XRD spectrum is identified by a hump centered at 2θ = 21.3°, which corresponds to an amorphous structure with a highly irregular arrangement. A broad peak at the same position of 2θ indicates a low-quality crystalline structure and the presence of –OH and –COOH on the surface of the CQD obtained in previous studies (He et al., 2018; Mondal et al., 2016). Therefore, the absence of crystallinity in the OCQDs, as opposed to the LCQDs, suggests a higher concentration of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface.

For the characterization of functional groups present on the surfaces of the LCQD and OCQD derived from lemon and onion juice, respectively, FTIR spectroscopy was performed. The results are illustrated in Fig. 4 . Particularly, the LCQDs display absorption peaks at 3523.95 cm−1, which indicates the stretching vibration of the O–H carboxylic bonds. An aliphatic C–H appears at 2920 cm−1. The distinctive peaks at 1722 cm−1 and 1552 cm−1 indicate the existence of a COO-vibrational stretch. Furthermore, identifying unusual stretching vibrations at 1430 cm−1 characteristic of aromatic hydrocarbons CC provides further evidence that the prepared CQDs contain an aromatic skeleton (Liu et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2015). The bands in the 1050–1211 cm−1 range display the existence of C–O functional groups. The peak of 1174 cm−1 reaches the C–O–C bond's stretching mode (Bandi et al., 2016; Hoan et al., 2019). The FTIR for OCQD shows a broad peak within 3100 cm−1 to 3600 cm−1, representing the O–H stretching vibrations of the surface poly-hydroxyl moiety and –N–H stretching (Yaghoubi & Nikje, 2018; Yarur Villanueva, Manioudakis, Naccache, & Majewski, 2020). An observable peak at 1413 cm−1 confirms that the carbon dot is attached to an amino group –NH2 (Yarur Villanueva et al., 2020; Dastidar et al., 2021). The peak at 1382 cm−1 is produced due to the stretching vibration between carbon and nitrogen. The presence of the nitroso group generated from the oxidation of amino groups in a certain percentage of the CQD molecules is suggested by the peak at 1593 cm−1. Furthermore, the 1120 cm−1 and 1056 cm−1 peaks define C–O bonds' stretching and bending vibrations in carboxyl groups (Mohamed et al., 2017). The functional groups on the surface of both LCQDs and OCQDs support that. The green synthesized nanoparticles are water soluble.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis study of the LCQDs and OCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties studies of the prepared LCQDs and OCQDs by ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra which measured between 200 and 800 nm, as shown in Fig. 5 . The aqueous solution of CQDs is dark (brownish) in daylight but exhibits vivid green fluorescence when exposed to UV-LED (395 nm), which is visible to the naked eye as displayed in Fig. 1. In addition, the as-prepared OCQDs solution exhibits a more pronounced green hue than the LCQDs solution when exposed to ultraviolet light, and it appears darker in daylight conditions than the LCQDs. The optical absorption peaks of both samples were detected in the ultraviolet region, with maximum absorption at 283 nm due to the n- π* transition of the CO band and a faint peak at 223 nm due to the π−π* transition of the CC bond (He et al., 2018; Hoan et al., 2019). OCQD's main peak at 283 nm is higher and broader than that of LCQDs, with a full width half maximum (FWHM) of 42 nm compared to 32 nm for LCQDs. A higher overall peak may result from a high concentration because the sample solution was darker. This could be potentially attributed to a deviation in the initial material and the formation of higher quantum yield of CQDs.

The determination of the optical band gap (Eg) for LCQD and OCQD was accomplished by utilizing the Tauc plot depicted in Fig. 6 . The optimal fit for both LCQD and OCQD, as shown in Fig. 6, is m = 1/2, indicating that the CQD dispersion undergoes direct band gap transformations. This is consistent with results previously obtained for CQDs produced via hydrothermal processes (Adsetts et al., 2020; Selvakumar et al., 2022). By facilitating radiative recombination, this direct band gap will enhance the efficiency and quantum yield of photoluminescence and electro-luminescence and encourage the development of high photoluminescence efficiencies. Two deviations from the optical band gap have been identified as the states responsible for the emission of CQD. The Tauc plots of both samples exhibit an initial peak at approximately 4.19 eV and 4.11 eV, followed by a subsequent peak at 5.17 eV and 5.05 eV, corresponding to LCQD and OCQD, respectively. The discrepancy in the energy band gap may be attributed to the quantum confinement effect, which is supported by the different sizes of both synthesized CQDs as verified by TEM, or it may be a consequence of the significant variations in functional groups between the synthesized CQDs, as confirmed by FTIR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LCQDs and OCQDs in strawberry packaging
ce:section.ce:para: Food packaging often utilizes films, which are delicate yet durable materials with functional properties. Direct application of these films to the food surface inhibits the proliferation of microorganisms, reduces food oxidation, and restricts enzymatic browning, moisture loss, and respiration of food items. The food may be covered by either immersion in the solution or application via spraying. The functional packaging can serve as a barrier against UV radiation and gas, thereby preventing the exchange of gases between the fresh product and its surrounding environment (Deshmukh et al., 2022). Furthermore, the perishability of strawberry fruits is attributed to their fragile composition and rapid metabolic activity during the storage period (Siedliska et al., 2018). Consequently, strawberries are highly vulnerable to tissue damage and infections caused by various phytopathogenic microorganisms, including viruses, bacteria, and fungi. Botrytis cinerea, often known as gray mold, is identified as the predominant pathogen affecting strawberries, with subsequent pathogens including Rhizopus stolonifer, Penicillium spp., Colletotrichum spp., and Mucor spp. (Feliziani & Romanazzi, 2016).

0.5 mL of each LCQD and OCQD was applied to a fresh strawberry surface to evaluate the packaging's impact on prolonging the fruit's shelf life. During the shelf-life investigation, physiological weight loss and decay tests were performed to assess the strawberries' quality. Throughout the storage period, the weight loss of strawberries consistently increased, as illustrated in Fig. 7 a. Strawberries packaged with OCQD and LCQD experienced a significantly lower weight loss than the control sample. Strawberries packaged with OCQD lost 5.21% ± 1.03% of their weight, while those packaged with LCQD lost 8.14% ± 1.09%, in contrast to the control group, which lost 18.71% ± 2.2% by the fourth day of storage.

Decay or rotting was determined by visual observation. The development of spots on the fruit and the softening and rotting of the fruits were recorded. The present study clearly identified that the decay percentage increased with storage time for all groups, as illustrated in Fig. 7b. Strawberries packaged with OCQD begin to decay after the third day. In contrast, decay starts earlier on the second day for strawberries packaged with LCQD and on the first day for the control group. Strawberries treated with OCQD showed a decay rate of 10% after three days, which rose to 11% after four days. The LCQD experienced a decay rate of 13.3% after three days, which then rose to 40% after four days. Comparatively, the decay rate in the control group was 66.6% on the third day and increased to 96.65% after four days, leading to complete spoilage by the fourth day.

Fig. 8 shows strawberries' color transformation and fungi development in a single sample from each group during the storage period. Initially, all fruits appeared fresh, but after two days, the unpackaged fruits (the control group) turned brown because of surface oxidation and the growth of a small amount of mold. After three days, mold became visible on the surface, worsening by the fourth day. However, fruit packaged with either LCQD or OCQD showed no evidence of fungal growth on the fruit surface after two days of storage, and the packaged fruit had a lustrous appearance. However, after four days of storage, mildew began to develop on the film-packaged strawberry. Compared to LCQD, OCQD remains crimson and shiny after four days, and a smaller mold appears on its surface.

This study identified Four genera of fungi in infected strawberry fruit tissues. The predominant colony numbers are Botrytis cinerea (52.18% ± 7.8%), Rhizopus sp. (25.27% ± 2.35%), Aspergillus sp. (8.8% ± 3.59%), and yeast (13.6% ± 5.3%), as shown in Fig. 9 .

Numerous studies support the claim that botrytis fruit rot, also known as gray mold, is the main pathogen impacting strawberries. The condition is caused by a prevalent fungus present in the environment. Spores can contaminate strawberry flowers upon landing and being in contact with free water during cold weather. Infections can result in flower decay, or botrytis may become dormant within floral tissues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifungal activity of the LCQDs and OCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles are a novel strategy for controlling phytopathogenic fungi in agriculture, as they exhibit potent antifungal activity against various phytopathogenic fungi (Hasanin et al., 2021). In this study, the antifungal activity of LCQD and OCQDs was tested against Rhizopus sp., Penicillium sp., Candida albicans, and Aspergillus sp. using the agar well diffusion method, as shown in Fig. 10 . The length of inhibition zones against various types of fungi was computed, and the values for each type of fungi were displayed in Table 2 . The results indicated that green synthesized LCQD and OCQD demonstrated encouraging antifungal properties against every fungal isolate that was examined. The calculated inhibition zone diameters for Rhizopus sp., Penicillium sp., Candida albicans, Aspergillus sp., and Botrytis cinerea for LCQD were 44.5 ± 1.1, 18.9 ± 0.2, 18.5 ± 1.7, 15.2 ± 0.5, and 20.2 ± 1.3 mm whereas for OCQD they were 40.5 ± 2.5, 12.6 ± 0.52, 18.8 ± 2.08, 17.7 ± 2.5, and 26.16 ± 1.5 mm, respectively. The antifungal properties of both CQDs are effective against various fungi, particularly Botrytis cinerea, a common pathogen that affects strawberries, as shown in Fig. 10. This makes them a promising option for protecting strawberries and extending their shelf life. The comparison of the antifungal effectiveness of OCQDs and LCQDs with different types of nanoparticles, as documented in existing literature, explained in Table 2. When comparing tamarind carbon quantum dots (TCQDs) derived from tamarind with LCQDs and OCQDs, it is observed that TCQDs have a smaller inhibition zone against C. albicans, measuring 12 mm. Conversely, the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N/CQDs) demonstrated a substantial area of suppression against Mucor and A. niger. However, both CQDs exhibit a substantial inhibition zone compared to the other nanomaterials listed in Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of antioxidant properties of LCQD and OCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant activity of both LCQDs and OCQDs was determined using 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl radical (DPPH) methods, mainly used for any nanoparticle that can be applied in industry. The results are shown in Table 3 . Both CQDs exhibit excellent potential for free radical scavenging activity in the present study. It is interesting to note that the antioxidant activity increased exponentially with minimal change in the concentration. LCQD and OCCD showed nearly 80% and 90% free radical scavenging activity at 100 μg/mL of the CQDs. It has been reported that the antioxidant activity of CQDs is derived from the hydroxyl group of the surface functional group (Ezati & Rhim, 2021). The DPPH* radicals are extinguished when the hydroxyl group is transformed into H*. However, lemon and onion juice lower free radical scavenging activity due to generating additional OH* radicals. The radicals restore stability and bind together, preventing DPPH free radical quenching capacity (Ezati & Rhim, 2021; Kousheh et al., 2020). There is a correlation between phenolic and flavonoid contents and antioxidant activities where higher concentrations of phenolics and flavonoids correlate with stronger antioxidant and antimicrobial activities. This suggests a synergistic or additive effect of these compounds in combating oxidative stress and microbial growth (El-Hadary et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3.7. the airborne fungal activity of LCQD and OCQD
ce:section.ce:para: Airborne fungi are the predominant causative agents of fungal infections in plants and animals (Wang et al., 2022). Botrytis cinerea Pers.: Fr., a necrotrophic ascomycete, is a significant source of gray mold in strawberry fruits, making it one of the most important illnesses. This airborne infection has the capability to induce the death of strawberry cells by generating reactive oxygen species and toxins, resulting in significant losses in output at all stages of growth, including post-harvest. Fig. 11 shows three 90-mm PDA settling plates exposed to air in a sealed box sterilized with OCQD and LCQD spray and the control condition. As illustrated in Fig. 11, the reduction or elimination of fungal colonies from the plates utilized for air spraying or cleaning with both CQDs is readily apparent.

Finally, it is worth mentioning that LCQD and OCQD exhibited substantial antifungal activity during the strawberry packaging experiment. The outcomes of the strawberry storage test and the antimicrobial test conducted are highly congruent. The direct application of CQDs onto the surface of fruits has been shown to effectively inhibit the development of fungi, decrease the rate of fruit oxidation (acting as an antioxidant packaging), and avoid enzymatic browning, transpiration, and respiration in fruit products. The functional packaging can serve as a barrier against UV radiation (as evidenced by the significant light absorption of both CQDs below 310 nm, as confirmed by UV–Vis analysis) and gas, hence impeding the exchange of gases between the fresh product and its surrounding environment. Finally, the water solubility of both CQD will allow it to be readily removed from packaged produce before consumption based on the functional groups on which both synthesized nanoparticles had explained before in FTIR. Ezati et al. (2022) found that the addition of CQDs to cellulose nanofiber as a nanocomposite layer improved the UV-barrier, antioxidant properties, and antifungal activity of the film. This resulted in an extended shelf life for tangerines and strawberries. In addition, Riahi et al. (2022) found that chitosan-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) applied to lemon have exceptional antifungal capabilities. The superior quality of onion-derived carbon quantum dots (OCQD) compared to lemon-derived carbon quantum dots (LCQD) may be attributed to their smaller size and more excellent antifungal activity against various fungi.

In this study, two solutions of carbon quantum dots (LCQD and OCQD) are synthesized using lemon and onion juices, respectively, as carbon resources in a simple hydrothermal process to generate Low-coat spray with antifungal activity for food packaging. Onions and lemons are ideal for making CQDs due to their local availability, low cost, and eco-friendliness. Their antioxidants improve CQD performance, though challenges like purity and extraction efficiency remain. Despite these issues, their abundance and sustainability make them promising, environmentally friendly CQD sources. TEM, XRD, FTIR, and UV–Vis were used to analyze the morphology, structure, functional groups on the surface of carbon quantum dots, and optical properties of LCQDs and OCQDs. The amorphous structure and nonmetric dimension of the prepared CQDs are 4.33 ± 1.67 nm for LCQD and 3.34 ± 1.72 nm for LCQD, as confirmed by XRD and TEM, respectively. The OCQD exhibits a darker, visible color and more significant emission when exposed to UV excitation, both perceptible to the naked eye. Furthermore, it possesses a broad and elevated absorption peak at 283 nm, which is discernible in UV–Vis, and FTIR validates a substantial number of surface bonds. These findings collectively support the claim that the CQD fabricated from onion is superior to that produced from lemon as the initial material. This study examined the antifungal activity of CQDs against pathogenic fungi via agar-well diffusion. Comparable antifungal activity exists between LCQDs and OCQDs against Rhizopus sp., Penicillium sp., Candida albicans, and Aspergillus sp, with 44.5 ± 0.01, 18.9 ± 0.02, 18.5 ± 0.17, and 15.2 ± 0.005 mm for LCQDs and 40.5 ± 0.55, 12.6 ± 0.05, 18.8 ± 0.2, and 17.7 ± 0.25 mm for OCQDs. Alternatively, the CQDs solution was applied to the strawberries using an atomizer spray nozzle to inhibit microorganisms and enhance their storage quality. Physiological weight loss and decay tests were conducted to evaluate the strawberries' quality throughout the shelf-life study. Strawberries packaged with OCQD and LCQD showed a significantly reduced weight loss in comparison to the control sample. Strawberries covered with LCQD and OCQD start to spoil on the second and third day, respectively. Decay begins earlier on the initial day for the control group. The high antioxidant activity (80%, 90%) achieved using a low concentration of CQDs can be used for the treatment of various cancers and food packaging. The study demonstrated that applying a layer of LCQD or OCQD can successfully prevent gray mold decay and improve the overall quality of packaged strawberry fruits.
No funding was received for this work.
The datasets generated during and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.
